For example:

```py
policies = [T.AutoAugmentPolicy.CIFAR10, T.AutoAugmentPolicy.IMAGENET, T.AutoAugmentPolicy.SVHN]
num_cols = 5
fig, axs = plt.subplots(nrows=len(policies), ncols=num_cols)
fig.suptitle("Auto-augmented images with different policies")

for pol_idx, policy in enumerate(policies):
    auto_augmenter = T.AutoAugment(policy)
    for col in range(num_cols):
        augmented_img = auto_augmenter(orig_img)

        ax = axs[pol_idx, col]
        ax.imshow(np.asarray(augmented_img))
        ax.set(xticklabels=[], yticklabels=[], xticks=[], yticks=[])
        if col == 0:
            ax.set(ylabel=str(policy).split('.')[-1])
```

Very nice! I have just a small suggestion for the changelog entry!

> @majorgreys so I argue that we don't need to go the feature flag route just because the fallback logic here is equivalent to what we had before so there would be no need to downgrade.

But the fallback flow is:

1. -- First request --
   1. Create a connection
   2. Send a request
   3. Try to keep connection alive
4. -- Second request --
  1. Try to reuse previous connection
  2. Connection fails, clear connection, raise an exception
  3. Tenacity retries
  4. Create a new connection
  5. Make request
  6. Try to keep connection alive
3. -- each further request --
  1. Same as for the second request


Is this correct? Is so, then the fallback logic is to raise an exception, then have Tenacity do a back-off retry, and then a successful request? Which is fine for a fail-safe, but a feature flag we can short circuit that exception -> retry logic for every single request.

Posted my comment at https://github.com/nipreps/fmriprep/pull/2894#issuecomment-1329308035, but meant to do it here:

> It doesn't seem to be new pip. Installing pip 22.1 in a Python 3.7 environment produces the same issue for me. I think there must be something in the dependency chain that's causing a resolution failure in 3.7. Pip's resolution mechanism is to find older and older versions of a tool, and we eventually found a nitime that is so old that it imports the entire module and breaks because numpy isn't a build dependency.
>
> I think the thing to do is to drop Python 3.7. [NEP29](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0029-deprecation_policy.html) recommended dropping it almost a year ago, and I assume that what we're seeing is the consequences of the broader ecosystem no longer testing on it. It's probably not worth a lot of effort tracking this down.
>
> I would suggest we take one of the following routes:
>
> 1) Adhere to NEP29 and drop Python minor releases along with the rest of the ecosystem.
> 2) Continue testing and advertise support for non-EOL Pythons until the fixes become non-obvious.
Many thanks!

Haven’t had much time to look at this lately (and probably won’t until the end of summer), but here’s an idea to address the TODO you asked for feedback on (collecting the built instances). The idea is to have the owner of the list allocate it, and have the step builder cooperate and fill that list.

The suggestion also starts taking care of the incompatibility between post generation hooks and this new feature, which will probably be necessary to land it.

```diff
diff --git a/factory/builder.py b/factory/builder.py
index ed39ebc..464a680 100644
--- a/factory/builder.py
+++ b/factory/builder.py
@@ -208,14 +208,18 @@ class BuildStep:
             parent_chain = ()
         return (self.stub,) + parent_chain
 
-    def recurse(self, factory, declarations, force_sequence=None):
+    def recurse(self, factory, declarations, force_sequence=None, collect_instances=None):
         from . import base
         if not issubclass(factory, base.BaseFactory):
             raise errors.AssociatedClassError(
                 "%r: Attempting to recursing into a non-factory object %r"
                 % (self, factory))
         builder = self.builder.recurse(factory._meta, declarations)
-        return builder.build(parent_step=self, force_sequence=force_sequence)
+        return builder.build(
+            parent_step=self,
+            force_sequence=force_sequence,
+            collect_instances=collect_instances,
+        )
 
     def __repr__(self):
         return f"<BuildStep for {self.builder!r}>"
@@ -235,9 +239,8 @@ class StepBuilder:
         self.strategy = strategy
         self.extras = extras
         self.force_init_sequence = extras.pop('__sequence', None)
-        self.created_instances = []
 
-    def build(self, parent_step=None, force_sequence=None):
+    def build(self, parent_step=None, force_sequence=None, collect_instances=None):
         """Build a factory instance."""
         # TODO: Handle "batch build" natively
         pre, post = parse_declarations(
@@ -246,13 +249,6 @@ class StepBuilder:
             base_post=self.factory_meta.post_declarations,
         )
 
-        # TODO: come up with a better solution
-        if parent_step:
-            if hasattr(parent_step, 'builder'):
-                self.created_instances = parent_step.builder.created_instances
-            else:
-                self.created_instances = parent_step.created_instances
-
         if force_sequence is not None:
             sequence = force_sequence
         elif self.force_init_sequence is not None:
@@ -275,21 +271,22 @@ class StepBuilder:
             kwargs=kwargs,
         )
 
-        postgen_results = {}
-        for declaration_name in post.sorted():
-            declaration = post[declaration_name]
-            postgen_results[declaration_name] = declaration.declaration.evaluate_post(
+        if collect_instances is None:
+            postgen_results = {}
+            for declaration_name in post.sorted():
+                declaration = post[declaration_name]
+                postgen_results[declaration_name] = declaration.declaration.evaluate_post(
+                    instance=instance,
+                    step=step,
+                    overrides=declaration.context,
+                )
+            self.factory_meta.use_postgeneration_results(
                 instance=instance,
                 step=step,
-                overrides=declaration.context,
+                results=postgen_results,
             )
-        self.factory_meta.use_postgeneration_results(
-            instance=instance,
-            step=step,
-            results=postgen_results,
-        )
-
-        self.created_instances.append(instance)
+        else:
+            collect_instances.append(instance)
 
         return instance
 
diff --git a/factory/django.py b/factory/django.py
index 25d6966..541e19b 100644
--- a/factory/django.py
+++ b/factory/django.py
@@ -246,13 +246,12 @@ class DjangoModelFactory(base.Factory):
                 "is either not set or False." % dict(f=cls.__name__))
 
         models_to_return = []
-        instances_created = []
+        instances = []
```

Hmm. You’re attempting to establish a global invariant about docstring
format so that your code doesn’t break. But you’ve provided no way to
enforce this invariant. The fact that you’ve needed to send PRs for
thousands of such changes across dozens of repositories makes it clear
that absent such an enforcement mechanism, regressions will quickly
be introduced. Yet I don’t think that we really *want* such an
enforcement mechanism. More tooling, more flakiness, more rules, for
little practical benefit. (And we don’t use Pylint, so there’s no “just
enable `extensions.docparams` and everything will be fine”.)

Internally, Google also has no enforcement mechanism for this. Some of
our core libraries have `Arguments:` docstrings—a minority, certainly,
but we’ve found no need to be stricter, and our tooling works just fine.
[TensorFlow doc generation also works with `Arguments:`.][tf]

It sounds like it would be much easier for everyone if you could just
make your own parsers more permissive. (Easier for us to not deal with
it, and easier for you so that you don’t have to keep dealing with these
regressions.) As you say, it should be easy enough. Could you give that
a try?

One of the things that I have learned from TensorBoard is that when
writing parsing/loading/reading logic, it is valuable to treat the data
as it is, not how you think it should be. :-)

[tf]: https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/compat/dimension_value#arguments

Regarding removal of `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`/`AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, not sure if this is a great user experience - so far, the premise was always that `awslocal` should ideally work out of the box, using sensible defaults where needed. I can see that this change could create some headache and break quite some existing pipelines out there. 🤔 (At least we shouldn't introduce it with a patch/minor relase imo.)
```
$ awslocal s3 mb s3://foo
make_bucket failed: s3://foo Unable to locate credentials
```

Could we maybe attempt to create a boto3 client, which will go through the credentials resolver chain, and then selectively only set individual environment variable defaults if the client cannot be created? Something like the following (not fully fleshed out, just conceptually):
```
try:
    boto3.client("sts").get_caller_identity()
except botocore.exceptions.NoCredentialsError:
    env_dict["AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"] = "test"
    env_dict["AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"] = "test"
    # optionally, we could try again, to ensure it works
    # boto3.client("sts").get_caller_identity()
```

This will add a tiny fraction to the runtime of `awslocal` commands, but I think it would make for a better user experience. Also, this way we could still make use of profiles and `~/.aws/config`. 

Thoughts?

This brings back memories from this deep debugging session leading to a Werkzeug bug report!

I don't have any big objection to the changes (except maybe the question about `_more_body`), and the `BufferedReader` will take care of optimising the `RawIOBase` generic implementations. Could it actually be faster in the end, as the buffer will be a `memoryview`? Reading the [implementation](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/main/Lib/_pyio.py#L1026) is pretty interesting. 

It really simplifies our implementation, and it feels nice to be able to use python built-ins.

I might have a stupid question: if I understood well, the flow is the following, starting from zero:
- we try to get data from the `ASGIReceiveCallable`
- we get data, but more than what the buffer (from the `BufferedReader` in that case) might be able to take
- we save the whole data internally, even what we have written
- next call, if we have data saved, we don't even try to receive from the coroutine but just return what we had saved, which might be 1 byte even if the buffer can take way more.
- bad cases: next call, we actually might hit the end of the coroutine, and will just return 0. Or we just had 10 bytes that we could have fit in the previous call.
- kinda good case, we have just under the size of the buffer, and doing anything above would not have helped. Or we still have way more than the buffer can take, and continue.

I understand that it might complicate the implementation a lot, or that it shouldn't be at all what we should do 😅
I'm also not sure what limits we could have to not take too much from the coroutine, because we might buffer way too much into the raw stream, but I'd try to get data if I have space in the buffer (which we can know if the `_buffered_body` is not `None`).
I also don't have an idea in what cases we fall most often. Do we often get a big body from the coroutine, and a small buffer from the `BufferedReader`, which would make sense to drain with multiple calls? Or are we more often with a big buffer able to eat the whole body?
The buffer into a buffer is playing games with me. 😅

Anyway, love that we're using more primitives like this! 😄

> Disclaimer: I am not familiar with autoscaler.

I read the PR description, and I have the following questions:

* Q1: What is the step-by-step process for killing a node? The following is my guess.

  * Step 1: **Autoscaler** makes a decision to kill a node based on the information from `GetClusterResourceStat` and `GetInstanceManagerState`.

  * Step 2: **Autoscaler** tells **Node Manager** to kill a node via `AdjustInstances`.

  * Step 3: **Node Manager** kills the node.

  * Step 4: **Autoscaler** knows that the node has already been killed by **Node Manager** by `GetInstanceManagerState`.

  * Step 5: **Cluster Resource Manager** knows that a node is killed by `ReportAutoscalingState`.

* Q2: Is it possible for Ray to schedule tasks or actors on the killed node between Step 2 and Step 3?

* Q3: Which kinds of information will be included in `ReportAutoscalingState`? 
  * [update] I found the definition in `message ReportAutoscalingStateRequest`.

Overall, things look good, except I'm opposed to the introduction of MediaQueryFeatureFlagDataSource.  I think the specialization of FeatureFlagDataSource into a MediaQuery* and QueryParam* variant adds too much complexity at the moment.  A multi-provider requires introduces a more ambiguity about where a particular feature flag is provided, and all TB surfaces need to include this "QueryParameter provider should appear the last" comment.

imo, the only thing we gain by having this split is that other TB surfaces can opt to include only queryParam flags without including the mediaQuery ones, in a way that does not require composition or redefining all the mediaQuery flags.  This benefit only applies if the other TB surface has no other need to compose/redefine feature flag data source.

If we renamed the existing `QueryParamFeatureFlagDataSource` to be `OSSFeatureFlagDataSource` or something, it would be reasonable for it to read from both query params and media queries.  Other TB surfaces might have to compose/redefine their own feature flag data source, but this is something that other TB surfaces will do already.

Alternatively, wdyt about having DarkModeSupportContainer be responsible for calling `window.matchMedia`?  I don't know if the media query result can change over the lifetime of the page session (if user changes the OS setting without refreshing the tab), but if so, maybe we don't need MediaQueryFeatureFlagDataSource at all?

Left some comments. My implementation review is finished.

So, this is correct:

```python
>>> ak.sort([1, 2, None, 3, 0, None], axis=0)
<Array [0, 1, 2, 3, None, None] type='6 * ?int64'>
>>> ak.sort([[1, 2, None, 3, 0, None]], axis=1)
<Array [[0, 1, 2, 3, None, None]] type='1 * var * ?int64'>
```

and this is consistent with the above:

```python
>>> ak.argsort([1, 2, None, 3, 0, None], axis=0)
<Array [4, 0, 1, 3, None, None] type='6 * ?int64'>
>>> ak.argsort([[1, 2, None, 3, 0, None]], axis=1)
<Array [[4, 0, 1, 3, None, None]] type='1 * var * ?int64'>
```

in the sense that the argsort can be used as a slice on the original array to effectively get the sort:

```python
>>> array = ak.Array([1, 2, None, 3, 0, None])
>>> ak.argsort(array)
<Array [4, 0, 1, 3, None, None] type='6 * ?int64'>
>>> array[ak.argsort(array)]
<Array [0, 1, 2, 3, None, None] type='6 * ?int64'>
>>> ak.sort(array)
<Array [0, 1, 2, 3, None, None] type='6 * ?int64'>
```

However, the fact that it's doing so by returning missing values in the argsort could be a problem for some applications. The way I would have expected this to go is:

```python
>>> ak.argsort(array)
<Array [4, 0, 1, 3, 2, 5] type='6 * int64'>
>>> array[ak.argsort(array)]
<Array [0, 1, 2, 3, None, None] type='6 * ?int64'>
>>> ak.sort(array)
<Array [0, 1, 2, 3, None, None] type='6 * ?int64'>
```

The two last items in the argsort, `2` and `5`, are the index positions of the two missing values in the `array`. You don't see the distinction when you do `array[ak.argsort(array)]` because whether you pick a `None` from the original array or you pass through a `None` in a slice with missing values, you get `None` in the output either way.

The reason people want to use argsort is to determine an ordering from one array (the result of computing a formula) and apply that ordering to another (may be complex objects). So, if there was some situation in which, say, `pt` had missing values but `eta` didn't, the (current) `?int64` output of argsort would differ from the (proposed) `int64` output in

```python
pt_order = ak.argsort(pt)
eta_sorted_by_pt = eta[pt_order]
```

With missing values in the argsort output, `eta_sorted_by_pt` would have missing values, which is strange because `eta` didn't. If the argsort output was strictly integers, some of which represent the positions of missing values, then `eta_sorted_by_pt` would not have missing values, just as `eta` does not.

Also, I think there's a strong expectation that argsort produces permutation arrays, and permutation arrays have no missing values. Even the integers with missing values removed from the (current) `?int64` behavior is not a permutation array.

As I understand it, the `Nones` are put into the output in a step that comes after descending into an option-type layout's `project()` result. For that, you only need to count how many `Nones` there should be and tack that many onto the end. What would it take to identify the locations of the `Nones` in the input (in order, a strictly monotonically increasing array of integers) and tack that onto the end instead?

Argparse help texts can't contain any restructured text markup, because it'll make the output of the `--help` CLI argument look bad, as it simply outputs it as plain text. That's the reason why the `ext_argparse` sphinx extension applies filters via regexes (which is the best it can do), so that the rendered HTML docs and man page can contain extra markup. There's already stuff in the help texts which doesn't actually belong there.

The user interface was in fact what was discussed so far but anchored to some place deep down in the implementation; that's backwards.

For instance here's one relatively basic user interface question I could not quickly find the answer to: let's say I want to update submodules of every west project except for one (too expensive) west project. Can I do this? If yes how? Should I entirely remove the west project from my west manifest or is there some other way? This is just one random user interface question that should be answered easily.

BTW I think `submodules: all/none` would look better than `submodules: true/false`.

Please don't get me wrong:  @kkasperczyk-no has done a tremendous amount of great work in this PR and prototyping very early is very important too, however for such a major new feature the bar should IMHO be high, not the least because there is no second chance with user interface design.

Hi - apologies for the back-and-forth, but I'm not actually sure this section is a great fit for the Get Started guide, at least not as written.

In particular, right now the guide directs users down one of two paths - either the first section if they're using `Model.fit()`, or the second section "with other methods" if they're using a custom training loop.

But this section doesn't fit very cleanly into either section - it's using tf.summary, which makes it more "advanced" than the first section, but it's also using `Model.fit()`, which makes it confusing to insert into the "with other methods" section, because the whole point of that section is that it's for when you are _not_ using Model.fit().

The most similar content to this that we have today is in the Scalars notebook where we cover custom metrics (although not batch-level ones): https://www.tensorflow.org/tensorboard/scalars_and_keras#logging_custom_scalars

My suggestion would be that we try to merge what you have here with that content into one place, that would covers how to use the tf.summary API with Keras to log summaries that aren't available by default with the callback - whether that's logging a custom metric like the learning rate example, logging batch-level metrics, etc.

That could just all go at the bottom of the Scalars notebook, or we could also consider adding a new page for it, like "Custom summaries in Keras".

I added some inline comments on the current PR. 

Other minor comments/ideas related to AreaDefinition attributes and properties:
- For AreaDefinition, many methods are not clear if they return data in xy or lat/lon; the pixel centroid or the outermost pixel corner. Maybe would be worth adding a common suffix (i.e. `_ll`) when they return lat/lons, and otherwise expect they return xy coords. This will enhance code readability. 
Example: 
   - `area_def.corners` currently returns the centroid lat/lon coords in the very old spherical object
   - `area_def.outer_boundary_corners` currently returns the corners lat/lon coords in the very old spherical object
   --> Possible new methods: `corners_centroids`, `corners`, `corners_centroids_ll`, `corners_ll` ... or see next bullet point
- Add utility properties like `upper_left_corner_ll`, `upper_left_centroid_ll`, 
  A possible pattern: `<lower/upper>_<right/left>_<''/'ll'>`  (or bottom instead of lower).
  Currently the `upper_left_corner `is provided by `upper_left_extent  `
- Add `xy_bbox `and `ll_bbox ` properties for consistency with satpy? Equivalent to `area_extent `and `area_extent_ll`
- Current `area_extent_<>` and `<>_bbox` follow the `(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax)` convention. More precisely the `area_extent` is defined as `(lower_left_x, lower_left_y, upper_right_x, upper_right_y)`. 
   - This is also the convention adopted by rasterio/rioxarray/shapely object `bounds` method.
      --> Maybe we could add a `bounds` property?
   - When retrieving the `area_extent_ll` (in lat/lon) the lower-left and upper-right corners do not necessarily currently define the outermost lat/lon coordinates of the area. Maybe this method should be changed! And maybe we could also add a `bounds_ll` method.
   - Additionally, the area_extent convention differs from the i.e. matplotlib cartopy and matplotlib `extent` which is defined as `(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)`
      --> Maybe we could add a `<mpl/cartopy>_area_extent_<''/ll>` property?
- For GEO AreaDef, all the above _ll methods currently return `np.nan`/`np.inf` values. Maybe ad-hoc processing using `get_geostationary_bounding_box_in_lonlats` could be implemented for `area_extent_ll`, but the corners would remain undefined ! Maybe would be worth raising an error to avoid downstream bugs !

While this is a useful QoL improvement for some users, I am worried that this change would promote unhealthy design choices. The strength of GraphQL is its typed schema. Creating a mutation that implicitly requires different combinations of fields may lead to user confusion.

We should definitely pick this up since the `@oneOf` feature will probably be added to spec, as that would resolve the issues of implicitness: https://github.com/graphql/graphql-spec/pull/825

However, this PR wouldn't support all use cases of `@oneOf`, e.G. nested `@oneOf`s inside of input types. I think the logic for this would better fit into the `Argument` handling.

If someone can point out additional examples of why this PR is still helpful, I'll be happy to reconsider. In the meantime, I think the technical implementation of this feature needs some extra discussion.

I think this situation (of getting to `otDump()` but not logging) is only applicable if dynamic log level `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_LEVEL_DYNAMIC_ENABLE` is used along with `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_PKT_DUMP` and the log level is set to value higher than default during run-time. 

If dynamic log level is not enabled, the `otDump{Level}` macro definitions will handle this: 

```c++
#if OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_LEVEL >= OT_LOG_LEVEL_DEBG
#define otDumpDebg(aRegion, aId, aBuf, aLength) otDump(OT_LOG_LEVEL_DEBG, aRegion, aId, aBuf, aLength)
#else
#define otDumpDebg(aRegion, aId, aBuf, aLength)
#endif
```

```python
from nidaqmx.system.watchdog import WatchdogTask
from nidaqmx.types import AOExpirationState
from nidaqmx.constants import WatchdogAOExpirState

def print_expir_state(phys_chan):
    ao_state = watchdog_task.expiration_states[phys_chan].expir_states_ao_state
    ao_type = watchdog_task.expiration_states[phys_chan].expir_states_ao_type
    print(f"state: {ao_state}")
    print(f"type: {ao_type}")


with WatchdogTask("cDAQ1") as watchdog_task:
    print("original state")
    print_expir_state("cDAQ1Mod1/ao0")

    new_state = AOExpirationState("cDAQ1Mod1/ao0", 1.0, WatchdogAOExpirState.VOLTAGE)
    watchdog_task.cfg_watchdog_ao_expir_states([new_state])

    print("new state")
    print_expir_state("cDAQ1Mod1/ao0")

    watchdog_task.expir_trig_trig_on_network_conn_loss = True
    watchdog_task.start()
    watchdog_task.stop()
```

I've took a liberty to push some minor fixes, mostly because it's much faster for me to make the change than to describe it.

Here are some general pointers:
- the lines shouldn't end with whitespaces. My editor removes them automatically, perhaps  you can set up your editor to do the same?
- the `..note::` admonition is quite prominent, so it's probably best to only use it for things that we really want to emphasize;
- the n-dash can be typed as `--`;
- there shouldn't be any explicit internal links -- instead we can use roles like `:class:`  or `:ref:`. These work better with versioned documentation (we have separate documentation for latest, and also for each major release).

Unfortunately, I think that we're going to need to go in another direction with this -- the Note and Chord objects are too strongly tied to having pitches attached to them to change that this late in music21's history.  Notationally, unpitched notes and pitched notes are very similar, but from an analytical perspective, having some notes whose .ps is meaningless, or Chords where "isTriad" doesn't mean that it sounds like a triad, is going to cause too many problems.

I think that the note.Unpitched object needs to be restored OR something similar moved to percussion.py -- we probably need to insert some layer between NotRest (which I wish I had called something like Stemmable, but it is too late now) and Chord -- ChordBase or something, from which a PercussionChord or something like that appears.

In general, when we go in a different direction, don't reuse names, so we shouldn't create something called Unpitched in another module that functions differently from the previous Unpitched object.
